messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.

As an AI assistant, I can assist in rephrasing the provided text using my own words. 

"Having the ability to adapt to change is crucial in today's rapidly evolving world. It is important to be open to new ideas and willing to adjust one's mindset and approach when necessary. Being adaptable enables individuals to navigate challenges, seize opportunities, and achieve success in diverse situations. In order to cultivate adaptability, it is beneficial to be curious, open-minded, and willing to step outside of one's comfort zone. Developing resilience and embracing a growth mindset can also contribute to enhancing adaptability. Overall, being adaptable is a valuable skill that helps individuals thrive and remain relevant in an ever-changing environment."

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I think this meta/cliché/portmanteau practice is fine when you're making a product or service for podcasting. For example, PodMatch, Castopod (2 points for this one!), and Pod Decks. Clichés aren't always bad, but you should try to avoid them like the plague.

In my opinion, utilizing meta, cliché, or portmanteau techniques can be acceptable when creating a product or service specifically designed for podcasting. For instance, consider the examples of PodMatch, Castopod (which earns two points), and Pod Decks. While clichés are not necessarily always negative, it is advisable to make an effort to steer clear of them as much as possible.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: While emoji can be fun for your cover art or even episode titles, avoid them in your podcast title. Some podcast apps still can't display emoji correctly. They might either omit the emoji altogether, or they would replace each emoji with “??” in the title, which can cause some unintended meanings. Any place that generates a URL for your podcast might also have problems or make a really messy URL in order to properly encode the unicode necessary to display an emoji.

Although emoji can add an element of enjoyment to your cover art or episode titles, it is advisable to refrain from using them in your podcast title. This is because certain podcast apps may not be capable of accurately displaying emoji, leading to potential issues. In some cases, the emoji might be omitted entirely from the title or replaced with "??", resulting in unintended interpretations. Furthermore, any platform that generates a URL for your podcast may encounter difficulties or create a disorderly URL while attempting to encode the required unicode for displaying emoji effectively.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If you use emoji in your title, you might have to always remind your audience not to type the emoji as part of your domain or social-network IDs. Nonetheless, they might try to search for your podcast with the emoji anyway, and how would someone even be sure they're using the correct emoji?

In the event that you include emoji in your podcast title, it may become necessary to frequently remind your audience not to include these emoji when typing your domain or social network IDs. However, despite these reminders, some individuals may still attempt to search for your podcast using the emoji, posing a challenge in ensuring they select the correct emoji variation.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Hashtags like “#podcasting” are pointless in titles and descriptions. They do nothing for your SEO and they will look weird (but not as weird as emoji). The only place hashtags are currently of any use is when you post on social networks, like Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, Pinterest, Mastodon, and even YouTube descriptions (but not titles). Each of these is a place where you would post about your podcast, not your full podcast content itself. So while it is good to hashtag the appropriate keywords from your title and description in messages you post on social networks, don't put the hashtags in your podcast title.

Including hashtags such as "#podcasting" in podcast titles and descriptions serves little purpose in terms of SEO and can appear peculiar (though not as peculiar as emoji usage). Currently, hashtags hold value solely in social network posts on platforms like Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, Pinterest, Mastodon, and even YouTube descriptions, but not in podcast titles. These platforms are suitable for promoting your podcast and discussing related topics, but not for incorporating hashtags into the podcast's actual title. It is advisable, however, to include relevant keywords from your podcast title and description as hashtags in the messages you share on these social networks.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: A good exception to this is when your podcast is about something known by its hashtag, and it's primarily known by that hashtag. For example, while Podcast Movement 2022 had its own hashtag, #PM22, that podcasting conference was not primarily known by that hashtag. But titling a podcast “#MeToo Survivors” would be instantly recognizable because it was known as the “#MeToo movement,” and thus this would be a good reason to use a hashtag in the title.

One instance where it is acceptable to use a hashtag in the podcast title is if the podcast is centered around a topic widely recognized by that specific hashtag. This is particularly relevant when the podcast is primarily associated with that hashtag. For instance, a podcast titled "#MeToo Survivors" would instantly resonate with audiences because it relates to the well-known "#MeToo movement." In contrast, although Podcast Movement 2022 had its own hashtag, #PM22, the conference itself was not primarily recognized by that hashtag. Therefore, utilizing a hashtag in the title would be justified in cases where the podcast revolves around a topic commonly associated with the hashtag.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This was very popular before 2018, and even I had recommended adding keyword-optimized taglines to your title and author tag. For example, The Audacity to Podcast‘s full title was “The Audacity to Podcast – how to launch and improve your podcast” and my author text was “Daniel J. Lewis, podcasting industry expert and how-to-podcast teacher.” That was an effective SEO trick back then, but some people caught on and then abused the system with titles that would look like “My Awesome Podcast – Entrepreneurship, Marketing, Passive Income, Relationships, Bitcoin, Business, SEO, and Vanilla Cream Soda.” That simulated title is 127 characters long, but I've seen some that were longer than 250 characters!

Prior to 2018, it was a widely adopted practice to enhance search engine optimization (SEO) by incorporating keyword-optimized taglines into podcast titles and author tags. An example of this approach can be seen in the full title of The Audacity to Podcast, which was "The Audacity to Podcast – how to launch and improve your podcast," accompanied by an author text establishing Daniel J. Lewis as a podcasting industry expert and how-to-podcast teacher. At the time, this served as an effective SEO technique. However, as some individuals began to exploit this system, titles started to become excessive, such as "My Awesome Podcast – Entrepreneurship, Marketing, Passive Income, Relationships, Bitcoin, Business, SEO, and Vanilla Cream Soda," which can reach lengths of up to 127 characters or even exceed 250 characters in some instances.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: There are some exceptions where you can include a short sort of “expansion” of the title. For example, Apple allowed our podcast about Once Upon a Time to remain under the title “ONCE – Once Upon a Time podcast,” and you can find some other, even high-ranking podcasts with similarly formatted titles. But if your title has to be explained, then it might not be a good title!

In certain cases, there are exceptions where you can include a brief "expansion" of the podcast title. For instance, Apple permitted our Once Upon a Time podcast to be titled "ONCE – Once Upon a Time podcast," and there are other podcasts with similar formats that have achieved high rankings. However, if your podcast title requires explanation, it may indicate that the title itself is not effective or compelling enough.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Podcast cover art is usually the first and most prominent thing people will see about your podcast—often even before the title itself! Visual space is limited (usually only half an inch or 1.25 cm square—or about the size of your thumbnail). So should you include a photo of yourself in your cover art? Here are some questions to help you decide!

The podcast cover art typically serves as the initial and most noticeable element that individuals encounter when coming across your podcast. In fact, it often catches their attention before they even read the title itself. Given the limited visual space provided (usually around half an inch or 1.25 cm square, similar to the size of a thumbnail), the question arises: should you include a photo of yourself in the cover art? To aid in making this decision, consider the following questions.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This episode is not sponsored, but I still want to promote my friend Mark Des Cotes from PodcastBranding.co. Please mention that you heard about him from me and I'll earn a commission, but I recommend Mark's personalized design services because I believe in him and his niche skills, not because of any potential earnings.

Although this episode is not sponsored, I would like to take this opportunity to promote my friend Mark Des Cotes from PodcastBranding.co. If you happen to reach out to him for personalized design services, I kindly request that you mention that I referred you. By doing so, I may earn a commission. However, it is important to note that my recommendation of Mark's services is based on my genuine belief in his skills and expertise within this particular niche, rather than any potential earnings.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: “Celebrity” and “influence” come in all sizes. You might not be known to the average person, but if you're known outside of your own audience within a niche, then it might be helpful to include your face in your podcast cover art.

The concepts of "celebrity" and "influence" extend beyond traditional notions and can be found in various forms. While you may not be recognized by the general public, if you have gained recognition beyond your own audience within a specific niche, it can be beneficial to include your photograph in your podcast cover art.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: One of the benefits of this is when someone recognizes you from a collection of other podcasts. That will make your podcast stand out more. Imagine what you would do if you recognized one of your favorite celebrities with their own podcast.

One advantage of including your photograph in your podcast cover art is the ability to stand out when compared to a collection of other podcasts. This can significantly increase the chances of someone recognizing you. Just imagine the excitement you would feel if you stumbled upon one of your favorite celebrities hosting their own podcast.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, if you watched the American version of The Office, you would probably instantly recognize Angela Kinsey and Jenna Fischer on the cover art for Office Ladies. In fact, you might recognize them so much that you would just assume it's a podcast about The Office because that's how you know them. And even if their podcast wasn't about The Office, you might still be interested to try the podcast because of how much you enjoyed those actresses.

As an illustration, suppose you are familiar with the American rendition of The Office. In that case, you would likely immediately recognize Angela Kinsey and Jenna Fischer on the cover art for the podcast Office Ladies. This recognition could be so strong that you might assume the podcast is solely about The Office due to your familiarity with the actresses. Even if their podcast had a different focus, your interest might still be piqued since you thoroughly enjoyed their performances as actresses.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, what is Joe Rogan's podcast primarily about? It's actually right in the title: “The Joe Rogan Experience.” It's about his experiences and perspectives through the variety of conversations he has with diverse guests. Thus, it's very fitting for the cover art to have his photo in it.

Take the podcast of Joe Rogan, for instance. The primary essence of his podcast, "The Joe Rogan Experience," is evident from its title itself. It revolves around his personal encounters and viewpoints, highlighted through a wide range of discussions he engages in with diverse guests. Consequently, it is entirely appropriate to include his photograph in the cover art, considering the nature of his podcast and its focus on his experiences.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: But when your show focuses more on the topic than on the person, your photo might actually be stealing space that could otherwise be used for a relevant visual element. And that visual element might better communicate the topic of your podcast and better attract an audience for that topic than your face would.

However, if your podcast places greater emphasis on the subject matter rather than the host, incorporating your photograph in the cover art could potentially occupy space that could be utilized for a more appropriate visual element. This visual element, aligned with the podcast's topic, might effectively convey the theme of your podcast and would potentially attract an audience with a specific interest in that subject matter, surpassing the appeal that your face could generate.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This can lead to people recognizing you at events before they hear your voice. This can also make it easy for them to see a gallery of speakers and know they want to attend your session regardless of what you say, only because they recognize and trust you.

This can result in individuals recognizing you at events based on your visual appearance even before they have the opportunity to hear your voice. It can also facilitate easy identification when browsing through a gallery of speakers, leading people to decide to attend your session purely because they recognize and have trust in you, irrespective of the specific content of your presentation.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Another consideration is if your goal is to connect more deeply with your audience. A photo literally puts a face to your voice, so it will be easier for your audience to relate with you. This can be extremely important for podcasts on more emotional subjects, like mental health, recovery, relationships, and more. Or if you're in a visually unique demographic and you're targeting that same demographic, like women, Asian, black, bodybuilders, etc.

An additional factor to consider is whether your objective is to establish a more profound connection with your audience. Including a photograph in your podcast cover art directly associates a face with your voice, making it easier for your audience to relate to you on a personal level. This can hold significant value for podcasts addressing emotional topics such as mental health, recovery, relationships, and similar subjects. Additionally, if you belong to a visually distinctive demographic and your target audience consists of individuals from the same demographic, such as women, Asians, Black individuals, bodybuilders, and so on, featuring your photograph can further enhance the connection and resonance with your intended listeners.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, I've seen a couple of people who simply don't pose well for portraits. So even if you hire a professional photographer, but your photos make you look like a creep, you might not want to use these photos. But instead of giving up, try finding or taking some better candid photos, or try different expressions. Maybe your smile—whether posed or genuine—just looks weird, so you could try a more serious expression, or maybe an action photo of you doing the thing your podcast is about (unless your podcast is about murder!).

As an instance, I've observed individuals who struggle to strike a desirable pose for portrait photographs. Even if you hire a professional photographer, if the resulting photos portray you in an unsettling manner, it might be best to avoid utilizing those particular images. Rather than abandoning the idea, consider seeking or capturing alternative candid shots or experimenting with different facial expressions. It could be the case that your smile, whether prompted or genuine, appears unusual, so it may be worth attempting a more solemn expression instead. Alternatively, for a visually engaging option, you can opt for action shots depicting you engaged in activities that align with the theme of your podcast (unless, of course, your podcast centers around murder!).

Rephrase the following text in your own words: And if you're working with a designer, always give him or her multiple photos to try, like multiple poses, all high-resolution, and as uncropped as possible. Mark Des Cotes told me he often finds the photo that works best is not the podcaster's first choice. And when you work with a skilled professional designer, like Mark, they can ensure the photo fits in nicely with the rest of your visual branding. For example, a photo of you in a Hawaiian shirt and straw hat would not fit well with a podcast about emotionally supporting your loved ones in the days leading up to their death. You don't have to go “cliché,” but you also don't want to visually clash.

When collaborating with a designer, it is important to provide them with a variety of high-resolution and uncropped photos to choose from, encompassing multiple poses. Mark Des Cotes shared that he often discovers that the photo that works best for the podcast cover art may not be the podcaster's initial preference. By working with a skilled professional designer like Mark, they can ensure that the chosen photograph harmonizes seamlessly with the overall visual branding. For instance, a photo featuring the podcaster in a Hawaiian shirt and straw hat would not align well with a podcast that focuses on providing emotional support to loved ones during the final days preceding their passing. It is not necessary to adopt clichéd imagery, but it is vital to avoid any visual contradictions or clashes within the podcast's branding elements.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The lighting, composition, and background of the photo can also make a huge difference in how you or a designer (like Mark) can use the photo. For example, a dramatically lit portrait that leaves a lot of shadows on your face might not coordinate with a predominantly light-colored cover art.

The lighting, composition, and background of a photograph can significantly impact how it can be effectively utilized by you or a designer, such as Mark. For instance, a portrait with dramatic lighting that casts prominent shadows on your face might not align well with a cover art that predominantly features light colors. The choice of lighting and the overall composition should be considered carefully to ensure visual coherence and compatibility with the desired aesthetic of the cover art.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: These have actually been my thoughts for several years, but I haven't done it before because I simply didn't like the thought of plastering my face in my cover art—as if it would seem conceited or narcissistic. But I think the benefits for me outweigh my personal reservations. So you might see my face show up in your podcast app soon! (But please do share your opinion with me!)
I have contemplated these thoughts for quite some time now, but I hesitated to implement
